data.title: Stitch Fix: The Worst Is Probably Over (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
It’s no understatement to say that the impending specter of tariffs, once renegotiated after the reprieve period, will disrupt global supply chains and reorder the world economy. And yet even with that said, though the stock market has bounced back from early April lows, many small and mid-cap stocks are stuck at penalty-box levels they likely don’t deserve.

Stitch Fix (NASDAQ:SFIX) is one of these companies. The stylist-driven e-commerce platform has seen a painful shedding of its customer base, but its declines are slowing alongside an often-overlooked return to profitability growth. The stock is down ~30% since the start of January, cutting deeply into a rebound rally that began in 2024.

I last wrote a sell opinion on Stitch Fix in January, when the stock was trading much higher at $5 per share. Now hovering just barely above $3, I believe a lot of the risks for Stitch Fix are already priced in. To that end, I’m upgrading my viewpoint on Stitch Fix to a neutral rating. Don’t rush in to buy here, but the stock has finally fallen to levels that are worth at least monitoring and putting on your watch list.

To me now, the stock is a more balanced mix of positives and negatives. To be sure, one major risk got added to the downside column for Stitch Fix: the risk of tariffs. Stitch Fix manages a complex supply chain, producing clothes under its own store brand while sourcing supply from various other brands that have different levels of exposure to recourse tariff rates that are presumably now being re-negotiated.

The risk of tariff hits, and the tough choice of balancing out absorbing the price increases and hurting margin or denting demand by passing on the increases to consumers, is likely to cause Stitch Fix to drop its near-term outlook. This risk is also stacked on top of an already-weak business, one that has been consistently shedding active customers for years.

And yet underneath these risks, there are glimmers of potential hope. Stitch Fix is gaining traction and seeing revenue growth in the men’s category - which, incidentally, is a bright spot in other more popular brands that are ceding share in women’s, like Lululemon (Stitch Fix, by the way, stocks a major rising Lululemon activewear competitor, Vuori).

And, in cutting down on performance marketing spend but maintaining the balance of stylist-client interactions that makes Stitch Fix still rather unique in a crowded e-commerce arena, the company is also making meaningful gains in profitability.

It’s also cheap, even though we expect hits to expectations and guidance. At current share prices just north of $3, Stitch Fix trades at a market cap of $398.0 million. Netting off the $229.8 million of cash on Stitch Fix’s balance sheet against zero current debt gives us an enterprise value of $168.2 million. This fact can’t be understated: most of Stitch Fix’s current market value sits in its cash balances.

For the remainder of the year FY25, which ends in August, Stitch Fix has guided to -6.9% to -5.8% revenue decline (adjusted for a tough comp against a 53-week fiscal year in FY24), and a 3.3-3.8% adjusted EBITDA margin, the midpoint of which implies $43.5 million in adjusted EBITDA.

This means Stitch Fix trades at just 3.9x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA - an almost unheard of valuation multiple, even with the recent stock market correction.

Of course, as just mentioned, Stitch Fix faces the prospect of tariffs that stack on top of the company’s slew of existing risks: but at the same time, its recent trend of profit growth (at a very cheap multiple of EBITDA) is promising. To me, that low valuation makes me believe that Stitch Fix is at or is close to finding the near-term floor for its share price.

Q2 download

Let’s now go through Stitch Fix’s latest fiscal Q2 (February quarter) results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Stitch Fix's revenue declined -5.5% y/y to $312.1 million, though this achieved a significant beat over Wall Street's expectations of just $298.0 million (-9.8% y/y) by more than four point margin. It's worth noting as well that Stitch Fix's decline improved meaningfully from a -13% decline in Q1.

One of the core issues for Stitch Fix is that the company continues to lose active clients, which it defines as somebody who has made a purchase within the trailing twelve months. The company counted 2.37 million active customers at the end of Q2, which shed 63k customers from Q1 and is own -15% y/y.

There is a silver lining, however. Revenue per active client has increased 4% y/y to $537, and has seen consistent improvement. The company notes that order frequency among its most loyal customer base is improving, which at least helps to justify the company's expensive investments into its signature feature: its stylists who help clients choose what products to buy.

In addition to this, the company is noting several segments that have returned to growth: its men's segment, as well as its "Freestyle" segment - which is traditional e-commerce from which clients can pick and choose their own clothes, without ordering one of the company's signature "Fix" boxes.

Stitch Fix's gross margins are also on an upward trend, up 90bps y/y to 44.5%. This contrasts well against many of Stitch Fix's peers in the retail industry, which have delved deeper into promotions during the holiday quarter to attract an increasingly promo-driven customer.

Of course, all of this is before tariffs kick in. Stitch Fix has a mix of brand partners and suppliers, but it's likely that a good chunk of its product is sourced from China, Vietnam, or other countries that Trump has labeled as "bad actors" that will get hit by reciprocal tariffs once the reprieve window ends. Still, we like the fact that Stitch Fix is improving its structural profitability - adjusted EBITDA roughly tripled in the quarter on a y/y basis to $15.9 million - which gives Stitch Fix a little more cushion to absorb a demand slowdown or margin hits once tariffs take place.

Key takeaways

I'm not necessarily advocating an all-out rush to buy Stitch Fix on this recent dip. I think the markets still need time to digest the latest tariff negotiations and see the impacts roll through as companies update their guidance to reflect the macro risks, and Stitch Fix is no exception. At the same time, investors should also recognize that Stitch Fix's huge fall from YTD peaks has left the stock quite cheap, while at the same time the company has several pockets of growth like men's alongside healthy profit levels. Keep a close eye on this name.

data.title: Booking Stock: This Travel Titan Quietly Prints Cash
data.description:
The travel industry gives me mixed feelings. On the one hand, it's an industry that has a lot to grow in the coming years (especially online bookings) with very favorable trends such as the expansion of people's incomes and the shift in consumer preference for experiences.

At the same time, it is an industry that is very sensitive to the macroeconomic scenario, since economic pressures make travel less frequent and less costly alternatives are chosen, affecting the companies' revenue, so it can fit into a “superfluous” industry that generally has a lot of competition and hardly any players with very relevant differentials.

It turns out that the case of Booking Holdings (NASDAQ:BKNG) (NEOE:BKNG:CA) manages to mitigate these risks and extract the best from this industry through wide moats.

Booking Holdings: Wide Moats + Great Trends

Booking Holdings is by far the largest OTA in the world, in terms of market cap, revenue, and market share. For instance, Booking's market share in the European industry in 2024 was 69.3%. And that's also because the company not only owns the Booking brand, but also Agoda, Kayak, and others, which already shows the consolidation of the company and the industry in general.

The competitive advantages revolve around the size the company has acquired and can be summed up by the term network effect. But to break it down, it's the company's power to have both millions of users and a gigantic base of hotel rooms and other suppliers, creating a robust and practically irreplicable ecosystem. From this, also derives other competitive advantages such as the ability to build customer loyalty with loyalty programs, such as Genius, and the company's ability to offer the best hotel prices due to its solid partnerships with hotels that allow for good deals, and due to its scale and efficiency.

The business model is also very positive. Net income is already very attractive, with a margin of over 20%, but free cash flow is even higher due to the dynamics of working capital (in some cases it receives the money from the customer and then passes it on to the hotel), as well as depreciation and stock-based compensation.

In other words, this business model allows for absurd cash generation, and consequently returns capital to the shareholder through buybacks and dividends, as the chart below shows. Note how the share count keeps falling quarter by quarter, even with a substantial SBC.

As the main player in this industry, Booking has a lot to capture from overall market size growth, i.e., it's not even necessary to consider further market share gains for the prospects to already be positive. For example, Sky Quest estimates a CAGR of 12.3% between 2024 and 2032 for the Online Travel market size, explained by global trends such as higher disposable income, expanding social media influence and changing consumer habits, for example, with only the continuing trend of migration from physical booking to online booking, Booking Holdings already has growth to capture in the coming years.

Key Risks For Booking Holdings

Even at this size, the risks of Booking's thesis should not be ignored.

In addition to the risks of industry slowdown due to macroeconomic factors and other regulatory risks, the key risks come from competition.

Well, in the section above I mentioned that its size guarantees bargaining power and competitive advantages, and this mitigates competition. However, this doesn't block the company from technological disruption, as Booking itself has done to the industry.

One possible threat is Google (GOOGL) through Google Travel. Even a loyal Booking customer can easily google the date of their accommodation and flight, seeing dozens of other players, and this also affects the paid traffic that Booking.com uses.

AI is also something that is constantly mentioned, both on the positive and negative side. In the earnings call, this topic was addressed as something interesting, as the company continues to invest in these initiatives:

“[...]We are highly focused on the many opportunities with AI and we'll continue the sophisticated work already happening across our company to integrate generative AI into our offerings, which includes Booking.com's AI trip planner and Priceline's AI powered travel assistant called Penny.[...]”

But there is also a downside. Personal assistants like Amazon's Alexa (AMZN) or Google's Gemini could eventually make booking more practical. At first, it seems quite intangible, since people like to see reviews, photos of rooms and not just ask the AI assistant to book the trip for day x at price y, but still, these assistants may become relevant in the (possibly) distant future, suggesting the best rooms according to some algorithm, showing photos, optimizing routes and so on.

As intangible as all this may seem, we can't completely disregard the risks, since Booking, as good a company as it is, is ultimately an intermediary (albeit a necessary intermediary).

Booking Stock Offers a Reasonable Margin Of Safety

Even with the craziness of the stock market lately, BKNG stock hasn't seen many dips. Even so, the company manages to present an attractive enough margin of safety.

To try to find the potential return for the shareholder, I've drawn up a scenario that I consider feasible (and even conservative).

As mentioned throughout the article, the industry still has a lot to grow over the next few years, possibly reaching a CAGR of over 10%. Booking also has the potential to maintain growth close to this, not only by keeping pace with market growth and consolidating further but also by coming up with new forms of revenue through the maturing of cross-sell and up-sell (insurance, rent-a-car, etc.). As the industry is cyclical, and part of this must be considered optionality, I chose to estimate a more conservative growth.

For margins, there is a good opportunity over the next few years. The company has already achieved a net income margin of over 30% but is currently close to 25%. For the next few years, a better revenue mix (with a model where the company charges and then pays the hotel) could increase the take rate, and greater efficiency in marketing could increase the margin. Not to mention the more organic part, such as continued operational leverage through scale and gains from automation and AI optimizations. I have therefore estimated a gradually advancing margin, reaching 35% as a proxy for “full potential”.

These assumptions lead us to an earnings yield above 6% in 2028, and above 8.5% in 2030. Although we can see that part of the growth is already priced in, this earnings yield for a company that remunerates shareholders well, has great prospects and wide moats, is already minimally interesting, but is made even more so by the fact that the free cash flow is even higher than this, i.e., the potential for shareholder remuneration would be even greater in this scenario.

The Bottom Line

Even in an industry that is complex to operate in and well impacted by macroeconomic factors, Booking has established itself as a high-quality company with good fundamentals, competitive advantages, and very strong execution in recent years.

Although part of the optimism is already built into the price, the prospects for the company are reliable and have room to continue to surprise positively if Booking's execution remains strong. For this reason and the expectations of a high shareholder yield in the coming years, BKNG stock deserves a buy rating.

The strategy returns shown are preliminary composite returns, subject to future revision (downward or upward). Please visit www.franklintempleton.com for the latest performance figures. Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. An investment in this strategy can lose value.

Performance data represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. Investment return and principal value will fluctuate with market conditions, and you may have a gain or loss when you sell your shares. Periods less than one year are not annualized. Performance results are for the composite which includes all actual, fully discretionary accounts with substantially similar investment policies and objectives managed to the composite’s investment strategy. Composite returns are stated in U.S. dollars and assume reinvestment of any dividends, interest income, capital gains, or other earnings. The composite may include account(s) that are gross of fees and pure gross of fees. “Pure” gross-of-fee returns do not reflect the deduction of any expenses, including transaction costs. A traditional (or “true”) gross-of-fee return reflects performance after the reduction of transaction costs but before the reduction of the investment advisory fee. The gross-of-fee return may include a blend of “true” gross-of-fee returns for non-wrap accounts and “pure” gross-of-fee returns for wrap accounts. Net-of-fee returns is reduced by a model “wrap fee” (1.5% is the maximum anticipated wrap fee for fixed income portfolios) which includes trading expenses as well as investment management, administrative and custodial fees. The model wrap fee used represents the highest anticipated wrap fee applicable to the strategy. Actual fees and account minimums may vary.

The Bloomberg US Aggregate Index is comprised of investment-grade, U.S. dollar-denominated government, corporate, and mortgage- and asset-backed issues having at least one year to maturity. Source: Bloomberg Indices.

Performance Review

Economic uncertainties triggered a flight to quality during the first quarter of 2025, driving U.S. Treasury yields lower and their prices higher. The economy continued to expand, while inflation remained higher than the Federal Reserve’s (“Fed”) 2% target. Meanwhile, President Trump’s plans for new reciprocal tariffs on U.S. imports led to concerns of rising inflation. All told, the overall U.S. bond market, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index, returned 2.78% during the first quarter. In contrast, the municipal (“muni”) market modestly declined over the quarter.

QUARTERLY KEY PERFORMANCE DRIVERS

Allocation

Security Selection

HELPED

Yield curve positioning

Higher quality securities

Overweight agency mortgage-backed securities

Bank/finance

HURT

Duration positioning

A duration that was neutral to slightly long versus the benchmark detracted from returns. Yield curve positioning was a slight positive for performance as the curve began to steepen. An overweight to agency mortgage-backed securities was additive for returns. Security selection contributed to performance given the portfolio’s higher quality bias and overweight to bank/finance securities.

Outlook & Strategy

The disinflationary trend may be interrupted as tariffs and retaliatory actions are implemented, but we expect inflation to move lower again over the longer term. Monetary policy remains restrictive. We expect central banks will continue to cut rates modestly in 2025. The Fed remains well positioned to provide support if the U.S. economy falters. Public debt levels continue to rise and yield curves may steepen given concerns over fiscal policies globally.

We see pockets of opportunity in developed market rates in Europe, the UK and Australia. While the overall uncertainty in the market environment necessitates caution, we do see some longer-term value opportunities in emerging market local currency debt. Spread sector fundamentals remain supportive, but valuations reflect those fundamentals and credit spreads persist at below historical averages. We continue to find opportunities within spread sectors and related securities, while remaining tactical. Developed market duration provides useful diversification.

Product Details 1

12/31/1998

Glossary of Terms

The Federal Reserve (“Fed”) is responsible for the formulation of policies designed to promote economic growth, full employment, stable prices, and a sustainable pattern of international trade and payments.

The yield curve shows the relationship between yields and maturity dates for a similar class of bonds.

data.title: Sempra: All Eyes On The Q1 Report Following The February Plunge
data.description:
Utilities (XLU) is one of just three S&P 500 sector ETFs up on the year. Even with the major bounce in US equities since last week’s lows, most areas of the market are down notably. Defensives remain in vogue for the most part, but it hasn’t been a smooth ride for all.

Today, I’m revisiting Sempra (NYSE:SRE). I last analyzed the company in February 2023. I had a hold rating then, and shares have returned –4%, sharply underperforming the S&P 500’s 30% gain.

I reiterate a hold rating. I like the dividend yield and valuation, but the chart is concerning. Amid weak momentum ahead of its next earnings report, and after a massive negative reaction to its Q4 2024 update earlier this year, I maintain a cautious stance.

Utilities Among Three Positive S&P 500 Sector ETFs YTD

Back in February, Sempra reported a weak set of quarterly results. Q4 non-GAAP EPS of $1.50 missed the Wall Street consensus forecast by a nickel, while revenue of $3.76 billion, up 8% from the same period a year earlier, was a material $970 million miss.

The management team lowered its FY 2025 EPS guidance to be in the range of $4.30 to $4.70 versus a $5.15 consensus forecast at the time. It issued FY 2026 EPS guidance of $4.80 to $5.30, along with a long-term EPS growth rate of between 7% to 9%.

Shares plunged 19% in the session that followed—its worst earnings reaction in more than three years, and it came after a strong 7.3% advance after the quarterly report last November. Looking ahead to the May Q1 update, the options market prices in a low 3.7% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle, expiring soonest after the May 6 report.

Looking closer at the quarter, the guidance is what hurt the stock. The downward re-basing caused the street to bring down their estimates. The reason? Adverse regulatory decisions in California, including lower allowed returns given rate case results in that region. Higher-than-expected expenses in Texas hurt, too, along with companywide elevated operating and maintenance (O&M) costs. Sempra has a history of issuing conservative guidance, however.

On the earnings outlook, EPS estimates have been brought in. FY 2025 is now seen near $4.62, with out-year estimates slightly above $5. Still, the bottom-line growth rate is seen in the high single digits over the quarters ahead, all while the dividend yield is 3.7%. Profitability trends are decent, but the sellside has turned very bearish on the earnings picture. So, Sempra is now a show-me story of sorts, in my view.

Sempra: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, Sempra now trades materially below its long-term average earnings multiple. That’s certainly a favorable backdrop, particularly if we see the EPS forecasts from the street come to fruition.

If we assume $4.80 of non-GAAP EPS over the coming 12 months and apply a 15x P/E, then shares should trade near $72, making the stock slightly undervalued. The dividend yield is also above its 5-year average, suggesting that SRE is a bit cheap.

SRE: Shares Inexpensive by Historical Standards

While I see the valuation as ‘good, not great,’ there are key risks. Additional unfavorable rate case decisions, particularly in Texas, could lead to further negative EPS forecast revisions. O&M and insurance costs are also on the rise in the Lone Star State. Also, there are ongoing competitive threats on the footprints in which Sempra operates. Moreover, natural disaster and wildfire risks are significant with the company’s assets and obligations.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show an unconfirmed Q1 2025 earnings date of Tuesday, May 6 BMO. The firm hosts its Annual General Meeting on Tuesday, May 13.

Corporate Event Calendar

The Technical Take

With a decent valuation and the sellside obviously quite hesitant on SRE, the technical situation is not encouraging. Notice in the chart below that shares fell hard to test long-term support around $60 just a handful of sessions ago. The long-term 200-day moving average is now negatively sloped, suggesting that the bears control the primary trend. What’s more, the shorter-term 50dma is significantly under the 200dma, which is another bad sign for the longs.

The earnings gap from February is the obvious chart feature. While SRE could penetrate that gap, there remains a high amount of volume by price up to the low $80s, which is a large amount of supply for the bulls to work through. For now, resistance is near $73, while support is in the low $60s. Hence, I see a range-bound stock for now.

SRE: Shares Near 52-Week Lows

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on Sempra. The valuation case is intriguing, but fundamental risks are front and center ahead of earnings. The chart, meanwhile, has its issues after a steep decline from late 2024.

data.title: Sunrise Communications: Attractive 8% Yield Makes This Swiss Telecom Compelling
data.description:
Sunrise Communications AG (NASDAQ:SNRE) is a leading Swiss telecommunication company that has an interesting service portfolio across mobile, internet, landline, and digital television. They've also recently expanded their high-speed broadband access using their own infrastructure. This way, SNRE has integrated its services with entertainment options as well. Additionally, SNRE now offers its services through a Device-as-a-Service [DaaS] business model, positioning itself as a tech enabler and content hub. The idea is that this approach will help them increase customer adoption, and in 2024, it seems to have worked to some extent. Still, I believe its main appeal is as an income play with its proposed dividend of 3.33 CHF per Class A share and 0.33 CHF per Class B share. In total, this amounts to CHF 240 million payables, excluding the typical withholding of Swiss taxes. So, I rate SNRE as a good "Buy" for long-term dividend investors at these levels.

Dividend DaaS Swiss Telecom

Sunrise Communications is a telecommunications provider of mobile, landline, internet, and digital television services. This means its usual clients are residential and business clients in Switzerland. SNRE was founded back in 2001 through the merger of diAx and Sunrise, and in 2010, they were acquired by CVC Capital Partners.

But more recently, in 2020, Liberty Global acquired Sunrise for $7.4 billion. This way, they completed the spin-off of Sunrise, and the company became an independent entity that currently trades under the ticker SUNN on the SIX Swiss Exchange (or SNRE on the NASDAQ). As for its business, SNRE's portfolio includes mobile services for voice and data, broadband internet with fiber-optic connectivity, traditional landline telephony, and digital television [IPTV] with TV channels and on-demand content.

One interesting aspect of SNRE's strategy is that it works through a business-to-consumer market [B2C]. But at the same time, it also has the Device-as-a-Service model, where the clients can lease smartphones instead of actually buying them. This business model benefits consumers with access to quality devices and connections, in exchange for a monthly fee for upgrades and maintenance.

And so far, SNRE's DaaS business model has led to customer adoption and retention. In fact, SNRE generated approximately 7% YoY sales growth with its DaaS business, which is actually a relatively impressive figure in this otherwise more stable industry. SNRE has also moved toward incorporating third-party entertainment offerings into its value bundle. They integrated OTT & Entertainment services, which are media services such as Netflix, Disney+, and Paramount+.

These third-party entertainment packages are now delivered directly over SNRE's internet platform. Clients have the option to bundle up TV, mobile, broadband, and streaming into all-in-one plans. Essentially, this move positions SNRE as an entertainment hub of sorts, positioning beyond a simple telecom provider. And in my view, this is a way of extending SNRE a little bit further down into the telecom value chain, ultimately reaching into what telecom services are often used for by consumers.

Furthermore, SNRE launched in November 2024 the Sunrise TV shop to manage streaming subscriptions, billing, and upgrades. Again, this is just a centralized platform that facilitates these value-added services to its customers, but also should boost client acquisition and retention. In a way, it should also help as a cross-selling and upselling strategy, which is ideal for companies like SNRE that already have a sizeable customer base.

Moreover, SNRE's business-to-business [B2B] market also added new clients. For instance, they signed deals with the media group Ringie, Formula 1 team Sauber, and Switzerland's largest retailer Migros. Similarly, SNRE now offers its B2B services to retailer Volenergy, the tech company EVAM, and the human resources provider Interiman. Also, several contracts were renewed with Swiss Federal Railways [SBB], Swedish-Swiss engineering company AFRY, among others.

It's also worth mentioning that SNRE's technology helps with new advanced solutions like 5G, Internet-of-Things [IoT], and private networks across several industries. A notable mention here is ANYbotics AG, the robotics company that develops autonomous inspection robots. ANYbotics basically equipped its robots with Sunrise IoT SIMS to enable real-time transmission of data to a control center for remote inspection and predictive maintenance. So, overall, SNRE is at the center of several telecom trends in Switzerland.

Naturally, SNRE does compete with the majority state-owned Swisscom in Switzerland, which owns most of the market due to its large infrastructure investments. So, I think substantially increasing its market share in this country will always remain challenging. Nonetheless, SNRE can indeed compete in terms of pricing and bundled third-party value-added-ons. And in fact, I think a good proxy for its overall market share is the overall Swiss mobile market share breakdown, in which SNRE has the second place with approximately 26%.

No Swiss Withholding Tax: CHF 3.33 Dividend

But for the most part, I believe SNRE's investment appeal is mostly as a dividend play. Recently, SNRE proposed a dividend of CHF 3.33 per share for Class A, CHF 0.33 for Class B, with a total payout of CHF 240 million corresponding to 66% of its 2024 earnings. And, according to SNRE's current dividend policy, they distribute up to 70% of their adjusted free cash flow. But obviously, these payments are subject to approval at the Annual General Meeting [AGM] on May 13, 2025, so there's still time to position ahead of their dividend.

Also, a quick note on their dual-class share structure. The Class A shares are the publicly traded ones under the ticker SNRE, and each has 1 vote per share. However, insiders and Liberty Global mostly own the Class B shares, and each of these Class B shares has 10 votes per share. In total, there are about 69.8 million Class A and 26.0 million Class B shares, so the Class B shareholders essentially control the voting power.

Nonetheless, the key advantage of these dividends is that they will not be subject to the 35% Swiss tax for five years. So this makes SNRE's stock attractive as an income-generating investment for shareholders because they receive full dividends without incurring further taxable claims. And since SNRE's Swiss market has a relatively stable and affluent underlying customer base, it makes these dividend payments mostly secure for long-term investors. But note that these dividends are still taxable by the IRS as usual.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

From a valuation perspective, SNRE trades at a $2.9 billion market cap, which I think is actually a bit cheap for a major Swiss telecom. Its balance sheet holds $387.7 million in cash against $4.8 billion in long-term debt. And they do have a sizeable Net PPE of approximately $4.0 billion, with their book value reaching $4.8 billion as of yearend 2024. That prices SNRE at a low P/B of 0.6, which seems cheap relative to its peers' median P/B of 1.8.

Also, with those inputs, I estimate its EV is roughly $7.3 billion. Also, according to Seeking Alpha's dashboard on SNRE, they're projected to generate $3.7 billion in revenues by 2026. This would be an extra $340 million in sales compared to 2024's figures, so I think it's fair to say this business is stable and has slight growth prospects going forward. This also matches the ongoing shift from slowly declining revenues from residential subscriptions being offset by increasing B2B and Wholesale.

The main reason for the slight residential revenue decline is that SNRE seems to be consolidating its older "UPC" and "Sunrise" lines into a single brand (for simplicity), but this does reduce their ARPU at least in the short term. But on the flip side of this, their B2B push to service corporate and SME clients seems to be paying off. Also, leasing capacity to other resellers (i.e., wholesale) contributes to offsetting the residential downtrend.

And in the next few years, I do believe the ARPU headwinds will subside, and their overall topline should continue trending slightly higher. So, overall, I feel this is a typical stable and robust company that should provide a long-term safe dividend for shareholders. Naturally, there are still some competitor risks, since its main competitor is the majority state-owned Swisscom. And in theory, if the political or regulatory environment turns against them in Switzerland, it could quickly send the shares much lower because there's little a company can do to defend itself from the state itself.

Fortunately, Switzerland is a historically stable country in that sense, so I deem this more of an unlikely tail risk to consider. Another risk is that telecoms in general often require major CAPEX to increase their service quality and reach, but also just for maintenance. And since SNRE is already heavily indebted, it does constrain its ability to maneuver to some extent. But other than that, SNRE's current dividend yield is actually quite compelling given its safe and stable prospects.

At the current 1.23 USD per CHF exchange rate, SNRE will pay approximately $295.2 million for its Class A shareholders. Each SNRE shareholder will receive 3.33 CHF in dividends (or $4.10 per share). Since the shares now trade at $51.50, that's essentially an 8.0% dividend yield based on today's prices and exchange rates.

Conclusion: Worthwhile Income Play

Overall, I feel SNRE's dividend yield seems quite compelling relative to the US's 10-year treasury yield of 4.38%, so I deem SNRE a "Buy" for income investors in particular. Its business seems relatively safe and secure in the long run, and mostly resembles typical oligopolistic telecom dynamics. While the shares themselves probably don't have much appeal from a capital appreciation angle, the dividend yield seems like a good option for extra yield. SNRE's dividend also has the additional appeal that if the USD weakens, its yield should increase by extension. Thus, on balance, I think SNRE makes sense in an income portfolio at these levels, especially before the dividend is paid later this year.

data.title: BBIN: A Conservative Bet On International Developed Markets
data.description:
ETF Overview

JPMorgan BetaBuilders International Equity ETF (BATS:BBIN) invests in a portfolio of developed markets equities excluding North American stocks. The fund has an expense ratio of 0.07%. This is slightly higher than the 0.04% expense ratio of the iShares Core MSCI International Developed Markets ETF (IDEV). BBIN has lower exposure to resources and energy sectors due to its exclusion of Canadian stocks. Fortunately, the fund has higher exposure to defensive sectors than other developed market funds. Together with its tilt towards large-cap equities, BBIN appears to have lower downside risk. The fund has comparable but slightly lower return than other developed market funds. Therefore, BBIN appears to be more suitable for conservative investors seeking exposure to international equities.

Fund Analysis

BBIN's portfolio construction methodology

BBIN seeks to track the investment result of the Morningstar Developed Markets ex-North America Target Market Exposure Index. This index is market-cap weighted and includes mostly large-cap and some mid-cap stocks from developed markets around the world, excluding North America. As can be seen from the table below, developed markets in Europe account for nearly 50% of BBIN's portfolio. This is followed by Japan's 23.7% and the United Kingdom's 14.7%. As can be seen from the table below, BBIN's exposure to these three regions/countries is much higher than other developed market funds such as IDEV and Vanguard FTSE Developed Markets Index Fund ETF (VEA). This is due to BBIN's exclusion of Canadian stocks. As the table below shows, VEA and IDEV have about 10.9% and 12.8% exposure to North American (mainly Canadian) stocks, respectively.

Source: Morningstar (accessed on April 14, 2025), created by author

Excluding Canadian stocks means lower exposure to basic materials and energy sectors

Since Canadian stocks typically have high exposure to basic materials and energy sectors, the consequence of excluding Canadian equities is that BBIN's exposure to basic materials and energy sectors is lower than VEA and IDEV. This is exactly the case. As the table below shows, basic materials comprise about 5.9% of BBIN's portfolio, whereas the sector accounts for 6.8% and 7.0% of VEA and IDEV's portfolio, respectively. Similarly, energy sector only accounts for 3.4% of BBIN's portfolio. In contrast, the sector represents about 4.7% and 4.9% of VEA and IDEV's portfolio, respectively. Basic materials and energy sectors are cyclical sectors, and they tend to outperform when the economy is in expansion but underperform in economic recessions. Therefore, BBIN's fund performance may be less sensitive to different cycles of the economy than VEA and IDEV.

BBIN is overweight in defensive sectors

BBIN has a high exposure to defensive sectors. As can be seen from the table below, defensive sectors such as consumer defensive, healthcare, and utilities comprise about 24.4% of BBIN's portfolio, whereas these sectors only account for 19.9% and 21.1% of VEA and IDEV's portfolio, respectively. Therefore, BBIN likely will provide better downside protection in economic and market turmoil.

BBIN has lower exposure to smaller-cap stocks

Additionally, BBIN has a higher exposure to large-cap stocks than VEA and IDEV. As can be seen from the table below, large-cap stocks account for 92% of BBIN's portfolio. In contrast, large-cap stocks account for 77% of VEA and IDEV's portfolio.

Source: Morningstar (accessed on April 14, 2025), created by author

Downside risk is lower

Since large-cap stocks tend to have better balance sheets and well-established business models than smaller-cap stocks, BBIN's high exposure to large-cap provides better downside protection in economic turmoil. Together with BBIN's higher exposure to defensive sectors, downside risk should be better than other funds. In fact, BBIN has lower downside risk in the recession in 2020 and in the correction in 2022 than VEA and IDEV.

BBIN has comparable performance

Let us now compare BBIN's performance to VEA and IDEV. As can be seen from the chart below, BBIN delivered a total return of 36.7% since its inception back in late 2019. This performance was slightly lower than VEA's 36.8% and about one percentage point less than IDEV's 37.7%.

Since these three ETFs have similar performance, investors may want to compare their expense ratios. BBIN's annual expense ratio of 0.07% is higher than VEA's 0.03% and IDEV's 0.04%. If BBIN had matched VEA's lower fee, it might have slightly outperformed VEA, rather than the other way around.

Beware of currency risk

Investors of BBIN need to keep in mind of currency risk. As can be seen from the chart below, BBIN's fund price has an inverse correlation to the strength of the U.S. dollar. A strong U.S. dollar can result in BBIN's underperformance. On the other hand, a weak U.S. dollar has the potential to result in its outperformance. We know that geopolitical tensions, and government policies such as tariffs, can introduce uncertainty and impact currency exchange rates. Therefore, investors should keep this risk in mind when investing in funds such as BBIN that owns foreign equities.

Investor Takeaway

BBIN invests in developed-market equities, excluding North American stocks. The fund has higher exposures to large-cap and defensive sectors than VEA and IDEV. While its performance is comparable to VEA and IDEV, BBIN has a slightly higher expense ratio and less exposure to resources and energy sectors. Therefore, this fund appears to be suitable for conservative investors seeking some exposure to international markets.

data.title: Fed's Powell Gives Stagflation Warning, It May Be Time To Worry
data.description:
Introduction

Federal Reserve ("Fed") chair Powell spoke in Chicago on the 16th, where he opened with the same line he always does, about how the Fed is in pursuit of its "dual mandate," and is focused on achieving its goals in line with the mandate.

For reference, the "dual mandate" that the Fed is always going on about is part of its founding ethos, and requires the Fed to strive for:

  • Stable prices (as measured by inflation)
  • Maximum employment (as measured by unemployment/labor participation)

One of the key levers that the Fed can pull is short-term interest rates, dictated by the Fed Funds Rate. They raised this rate when COVID-19 supply chain and "helicopter money" stimulus policy caused US inflation to grow and ultimately peak at over 8%.

Inflation has fallen in response to these rate hikes, and so the Fed has since lowered rates. We are down 100bp from the peak, but have not moved for a few months, as the Fed has preferred to stay rates where they are so they can "wait and see" what comes next.

See how inflation peaks the moment the Fed starts to act in 2022:

Inflation today is now being driven by other factors, as the rate hikes cleared out significant amounts of damage done by the past drivers.

This brings us to Powell's speech at the Economics Club, where after he discussed the dual mandate's importance, he discussed what may shift the balance the Fed believes they have facilitated in the labor market and with prices.

Powell's Speech

For those who missed my last piece of "Fedwatching," I like to break down what Powell says in his speeches because he's become known as a very prudent and purposeful speaker, meaning that what he says often is translated directly into policy, even when he's wrong, paging Team Transitory.

For those wanting to see all of my macroeconomics and Fed coverage, you can search the SPX ticker on my Seeking Alpha profile.

Let's get to the speech. Powell starts, as we covered, by discussing his dual mandate.

At the Fed, we are always focused on the dual-mandate goals given to us by Congress: maximum employment and stable prices. Despite heightened uncertainty and downside risks, the U.S. economy is still in a solid position. The labor market is at or near maximum employment. Inflation has come down a great deal but is running a bit above our 2 percent objective.

Two percent inflation is considered "natural" by economists, and healthy for an economy as it discourages saving and encourages consumption by a population, promoting growth, but doesn't erode buying power so much that it robs the population of their ability to consume.

It's a tight rope that one must walk delicately.

Current CPI and PCE, the two most common measures of inflation, are, in fact, elevated where Powell says they are, and the FOMC, the Fed's committee that decides how the central rate will change over time, is adjusting their expectations of inflation and growth due to new players on the field that weren't there back in December.

We can see this in the March FOMC meeting projections, which show revisions in real GDP, unemployment, inflation (which they use PCE to measure), and the expectations for the Fed Funds Rate. I've highlighted all the revisions over the December numbers:

The next release of these projections will come in early May, and I'll be very ready to see how they change now that we've seen much more of the new US trade policy following President Trump's "Liberation Day."

There may be some worry before then. I've been sounding the alarm bells about April 30th, or the day that the BEA announces Q1 GDP data. Projections from the Fed currently see the US contracting by almost 2.5%, which is startling. Of course, Powell addressed this next in his speech, following the basically-mandated (pun intended) mention of the Fed's purpose.

Powell continues:

Turning to the incoming data, we will get the initial reading on first-quarter GDP in a couple of weeks. The data in hand so far suggest that growth has slowed in the first quarter from last year's solid pace. Despite strong motor vehicle sales, overall consumer spending appears to have grown modestly. In addition, strong imports during the first quarter, reflecting attempts by businesses to get ahead of potential tariffs, are expected to weigh on GDP growth.

Importers causing the whole of GDP contraction has been a theme of mine since I started writing about this negative GDP forecast over a month ago. That being said, knowing the cause of the contraction doesn't mean that we are able to stop it. I believe that markets are pricing in less pain than will actually be there, especially given how low the estimated dropped at the beginning of the month, over 3.5% at some point.

Powell goes on regarding this data, which is made up entirely of surveys, or "soft data" as Powell as called it in the past.

Surveys of households and businesses report a sharp decline in sentiment and elevated uncertainty about the outlook...Outside forecasts for the full year are coming down and...point to continued slowing but still positive growth...

In the labor market...job growth has slowed relative to last year, the combination of low layoffs and lower labor force growth has kept the unemployment rate in a low and stable range. Meanwhile, the ratio of job openings to unemployed job seekers has remained just above 1, near its pre-pandemic level. Wage growth has continued to moderate while still outpacing inflation. Overall, the labor market appears to be in solid condition and broadly in balance and is not a significant source of inflationary pressure.

Recent jobs reports have been positive, and unemployment is at levels considered natural, or at "maximum employment." This means that folks currently unemployed are primarily folks who are frictionally unemployed, or those who are "between jobs." This kind of unemployment is typically temporary, and churning, meaning that folks are not individually put out of the labor pool for too long. That behavior is normal for a capitalist economy, where folks can pick and choose their work, or choose to leave the labor force altogether.

While this figure is relatively elevated from the last few years following the COVID-19 pandemic, this current level is still considered natural.

Powell continues:

As for our price-stability mandate, inflation has significantly eased from its pandemic highs of mid-2022 without the kind of painful rise in unemployment that has frequently accompanied efforts to bring down high inflation. Progress on inflation continues at a gradual pace, and recent readings remain above our 2 percent objective...

Recent CPI reports corroborate this, and Powell must be happy with the progress they've made on inflation, which is why rates are being held as they are.

The Speech Shifts

Powell turns his attention suddenly to the elephant in the room:

Looking forward, the new Administration is in the process of implementing substantial policy changes in four distinct areas: trade, immigration, fiscal policy, and regulation.

Those policies are still evolving, and their effects on the economy remain highly uncertain. As we learn more, we will continue to update our assessment. The level of the tariff increases announced so far is significantly larger than anticipated. The same is likely to be true of the economic effects, which will include higher inflation and slower growth. Both survey- and market-based measures of near-term inflation expectations have moved up significantly, with survey participants pointing to tariffs. Survey measures of longer-term inflation expectations, for the most part, appear to remain well anchored; market-based breakevens continue to run close to 2 percent.

The key wording there was something that Powell has refused to say up until now, the first time he's speaking after having time to digest the severity and totality of the new "US vs. Everyone" trade war paradigm that President Trump has catalyzed. This new wording is found in this line:

...higher inflation and slower growth.

The word that Powell would never use, because he's too careful and worrisome about how markets will interpret him, that means exactly that phrase, is "stagflation."

This is a period of stagnant growth despite higher prices. Normally, when prices rise, the result is growth in the underlying business. However, if prices rise and the difference goes to the government in the form of a duty, we don't see growth in the underlying business. Usually, we see the opposite, with lower demand at higher prices compounding the effect. However, prices will still rise, as they are artificially inflated by tariffs. This lower demand, but drives up prices, leading to this kind of situation potentially developing.

This is not a good situation for the average consumer, as one can imagine. Compound this with new tariff levels that are effectively embargos on some goods, especially those de minimis goods now subject to $200 flat fees, and the now 145% blanket tariff across all China goods (except those being exempted after the fact, like smartphones).

One thing I don't understand about this whole situation with the Chinese tariffs, is that if we exempt smartphones, but not smartphone components, then we are giving the competitive advantage to Chinese manufacturers and a disadvantage to those assembling phones here, who would need to source the components from China.

Regardless, and without pausing too long on the stagflation comment (which still roiled markets anyway), Powell continues:

As we gain a better understanding of the policy changes, we will have a better sense of the implications for the economy, and hence for monetary policy. Tariffs are highly likely to generate at least a temporary rise in inflation. The inflationary effects could also be more persistent. Avoiding that outcome will depend on the size of the effects, on how long it takes for them to pass through fully to prices, and, ultimately, on keeping longer-term inflation expectations well anchored.

Our obligation is to keep longer-term inflation expectations well anchored and to make certain that a one-time increase in the price level does not become an ongoing inflation problem. As we act to meet that obligation, we will balance our maximum-‑employment and price-stability mandates, keeping in mind that, without price stability, we cannot achieve the long periods of strong labor market conditions that benefit all Americans. We may find ourselves in the challenging scenario in which our dual-mandate goals are in tension. If that were to occur, we would consider how far the economy is from each goal, and the potentially different time horizons over which those respective gaps would be anticipated to close.

What was interesting about this last paragraph, which comes just before he concludes his speech, was the priority list he gave us:

...without price stability, we cannot achieve the long periods of strong labor market conditions...

Putting the focus on prices first is important to note, because it means that the Fed is likely to wait for changes to fully materialize and stabilize at higher levels before they act. It is not, however, dovish at all. This was interpreted by the market as being very hawkish, as Powell effectively says that he can't lower rates yet.

Currently, the market believes that the Fed will hold rates at its next meeting on May 7th.

However, the market is expecting our next rate cut to come in June, the meeting after, with a little under a 3rd of participants making bets that the rate will stay even at the June meeting.

But if Powell is to be believed here, there is a good chance that the 32% of folks betting 425-450bp, i.e., rates will stay level in June, are right. If we see the negative GDP reading, indicating slowing growth, but prices don't come down, Powell won't be able to lower rates without the threat of more inflation.

Conclusion

Ultimately, Powell gave us a clear look inside the Fed's FOMC post-Liberation Day, where he's been able to fully digest the tariff news. He and the FOMC are worried about stagflation, effectively calling out in this latest speech. They worry that a rise in prices and a slowing of growth happening at the same time will force them to hold rates steady, even if the labor market warrants cuts. Powell made it clear that the Fed won't be making moves until it has all the data, reiterating former calls for "hard data" before they act.

In his own conclusion, Powell remarked:

For the time being, we are well positioned to wait for greater clarity before considering any adjustments to our policy stance. We continue to analyze the incoming data, the evolving outlook, and the balance of risks. We understand that elevated levels of unemployment or inflation can be damaging and painful for communities, families, and businesses. We will continue to do everything we can to achieve our maximum-employment and price-stability goals.

It seems that they still intend to act prudently, but that may subvert the expectations of markets. While markets believe that there are four rate cuts coming this year, a total 100bp decline, I foresee a much slower pace based on this commentary from Powell today, likely closer to 50bp, and likely farther out in the year as the Fed "waits for greater clarity" regarding the White House's ever-changing trade policy.

Don't expect rate cuts anytime soon. This has helped spur on my cash conviction.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Precious Metals Royalty And Streaming Companies: The March Report
data.description:
Precious metals royalty and streaming companies represent a very interesting sub-industry of the precious metals mining industry. They provide some leverage to the growing metals prices, similar to the typical mining companies; however, they are less risky in comparison to them. Their incomes are derived from royalty and streaming agreements. Under a metal streaming agreement, the streaming company provides an upfront payment to acquire the right to future deliveries of a predefined percentage of metal production of a mining operation.

The streaming company also pays some ongoing payments that are usually well below the market price of the metal. They can be set as a fixed sum (e.g., $300/toz gold) or as a percentage (e.g., 20% of the prevailing gold price), or a combination of both (e.g., the lower of a) $300/toz gold and b) 20% of the prevailing gold price). The royalties usually apply to a small fraction of the mining project production (usually 1-3%), and they are not connected with ongoing payments. They can have various forms, but the most common is a small percentage of the net smelter return ("NSR"). The NSR is calculated as revenues from the sale of the mined products minus transportation and refining costs.

To better track the overall performance of the whole sub-industry, I created a capitalization-weighted index (the Precious Metals Royalty and Streaming Index) consisting of 11 companies (in June 2020, expanded to 15). Later, based on the inquiries of readers, I also introduced an equal-weighted version of the index. Until March 2021, both indices included the same companies and were calculated back to January 2019.

However, some major changes occurred in April 2021. Due to the boom of the royalty and streaming industry and the emergence of many new companies, the indices experienced two major changes. First of all, the market capitalization-weighted index was modified to include only the 5 biggest companies: Franco-Nevada (FNV), Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM), Royal Gold (RGLD), Osisko Gold Royalties (OR), and Sandstorm Gold (SAND). The combined weight of these 5 companies on the old index was around 95%; therefore, the small companies had only a negligible impact on their performance. The values of the index were re-calculated back to January 2019, and between January 2019 and March 2021, the difference in the overall performance of the old and the new index was only 2.29 percentage points. The second change is related to the equally weighted index that was expanded to 20 companies.

The previous editions of the monthly report can be found here: May 2019, June 2019, July 2019, August 2019, September 2019, October 2019, November 2019, December 2019, January 2020, February 2020, March 2020, April 2020, May 2020, June 2020, July 2020, August 2020, September 2020, October 2020, November 2020, December 2020, January 2021, February 2021, March 2021, April 2021, May 2021, June 2021, July 2021, August 2021, September 2021, October 2021, November 2021, December 2021, January 2022, February 2022, March 2022, April 2022, May 2022, June 2022, July 2022, August 2022, September 2022, October 2022, November 2022, December 2022, January 2023, February 2023, March 2023, April 2023, May 2023, June 2023, July 2023, August 2023, September 2023, October 2023, November 2023, December 2023, January 2024, February 2024, March 2024, April 2024, May 2024, June 2024, July 2024, August 2024, September 2024, October 2024, November 2024, December 2024, January 2025, February 2025.

Wheaton Precious Metals remains the biggest precious metals R&S company, with a market capitalization of more than $35 billion. Second-placed Franco-Nevada had a market capitalization slightly above $30 billion as of the end of March. At the opposite end of the ranking remains Silver Crown Royalties, with a market capitalization of $10.7 million. No major changes occurred in the ranking in March, only Elemental Altus Royalties (OTCQX:ELEMF) and EMX Royalty (EMX) outgrew Orogen Royalties (OTCQX:OGNRF).

March was a successful month for the precious metals R&S companies. Only 3 out of 17 ended in red numbers. The rest of the industry ended the month with some very nice gains. The highest ones recorded Sandstorm Gold (23.16%). Its share price was boosted not only by the growing gold price but also by positive portfolio updates. Very nice gains close to 20% recorded also Vox Royalty (VOXR) and Empress Royalty (OTCQX:EMPYF). The highest decline was experienced by Silver Crown Royalties (SCRI:CA). Its share price declined by 5.91%, prolonging the bearish trend that started back in February.

The precious metals prices recorded an impressive growth in March. As a result, the share price of the SPDR Gold Trust ETF (GLD) grew by 9.45%, and the iShares Silver Trust ETF (SLV) grew by 9.47%. This provided a strong boost to shares of gold miners. The VanEck Vectors Gold Miners ETF (GDX) and the VanEck Vectors Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ) grew by 15.74% and 17.55% respectively. The precious metals R&S companies did slightly worse than the miners, but in line with the precious metals. The Precious Metals R&S Index grew by 11.93%, and the Precious Metals R&S Equally Weighted Index grew by 9.1%.

The March News

The March news was mostly related to the Q4 financial results and portfolio updates. The only deal worth mentioning is Triple Flag's acquisition of the 5% gold and silver stream on Arcata and Azuca mines.

Franco-Nevada reported Q4 revenues of $317.9 million, operating cash flow of $243 million, and net income of $175.4 million. All three numbers represent notable improvement not only to the previous quarter but also to the same period of the previous year. However, they were unable to beat the Q2 2023 numbers, as the increased gold prices were unable to fully compensate for the loss of Cobre Panama production. As of the end of Q4, Franco-Nevada held cash of $1.45 billion, and it was debt-free. In 2025, Franco-Nevada expects sales of 465,000-525,000 toz of gold equivalent, which is more than 463,334 toz sold in 2024. However, the precious metals segment should experience growth, from 355,310 toz in 2024 to 385,000-425,000 toz in 2025. By 2028, the volume of precious metals sales should grow to 400,000-440,000 toz of gold equivalent, while the overall volume of sales should grow to 505,000-565,000 toz of gold equivalent.

Wheaton Precious Metals reported Q4 revenues of $380.5 million, operating cash flow of $319.5 million, and net income of $88.1 million. The net income declined by 43% compared to the previous quarter, due to a $109 million impairment charge related to the Voisey's Bay cobalt stream. However, both revenues and operating cash flow reached new record highs. Wheaton held cash of $818.2 million, and it was virtually debt-free as of the end of Q4. According to the 2025 guidance, Wheaton should record attributable production of 600,000-670,000 toz of gold equivalent. By 2029, the volume should grow to the 870,000 toz level.

Wheaton also declared a dividend of $0.165 per share, which is a 6.5% increase to the previous dividend of $0.155 per share. The dividend date was set to April 11, with a record date of April 1.

On April 4, Wheaton announced the winner of its inaugural Future of Mining Challenge. ReThink Milling Inc. was awarded $1 million for its Conjugate Anvil Hammer Mill (CAHM) and MonoRoll technologies, which have the potential to revolutionize the milling process, by significantly lowering energy usage.

Royal Gold provided the 2025 sales guidance. The company expects the sales volumes to be 210,000-230,000 toz gold, 2.7-3.3 million toz silver, 13.5-16 million lb copper, and $18-21 million in the form of other metals. Royal Gold also expects the DD&A to range from $126 to $142 million, and the effective tax rate to be 17-22%.

Triple Flag Precious Metals (TFPM) acquired a 5% gold and silver stream Sierra Sun Precious Metals' Peruvian Arcata and Azuca mines for $35 million. The Arcata mine should be restarted soon, and the attributable production volume should increase to 5,000-6,000 toz of gold equivalent by 2028. The Azuca mine should be restarted by the end of 2029. The combined mine life of the two mines is at least 10 years.

Sandstorm Gold provided a portfolio update. The most important news is that Erdene Resource Development's (OTCPK:ERDCF) Mongolian Bayan Khundii gold mine should start production in the middle of 2025. Sandstorm holds a 1% NSR royalty on Bayan Khundii. Some great drill intersections were made at Fruta del Norte (173 g/t gold over 8.5 meters at New Trancaloma Target), 4.48 g/t gold over 57 meters at Omai Gold Mines' (OTCQB:OMGGF) Wenot deposit, and 465.5 meters grading 1.19% copper and 0.56 g/t gold at the Hugo North Extension of the Oyu Tolgoi mine.

The company also announced a quarterly dividend of C$0.02 ($0.014) that will be paid on April 25 to shareholders of record as of April 14. Sandstorm also announced the renewal of its Normal Course Issuer Bid and Automatic Share Purchase Plan.

On March 31, Sandstorm announced that SSR Mining budgeted $60-100 million for early-works capital investments at Hod Maden. Based on this information, Sandstorm maintains its estimate that the mine should get into production in 2028. Sandstorm holds a 2% NSR royalty, and a 20% gold stream on Hod Maden.

Gold Royalty (GROY) reported Q4 revenues of $3.4 million, operating cash flow of $1.3 million, and net income of -$3.2 million. Both revenues and operating cash flow reached new record highs. However, the company recorded a loss of $3.2 million. The company ended Q4 with cash of $2.5 million, and net debt of $47.5 million. According to the 2025 guidance, Gold Royalty expects sales of 5,700-7,000 toz of gold equivalent, which compares favorably to the 2024 sales of 5,462 toz. However, the longer-term growth profile is much more interesting, as Gold Royalty projects sales of 23,000-28,000 toz of gold equivalent for 2029.

Metalla Royalty & Streaming (MTA) generated revenues of $2.1 million, operating cash flow of -$0.4 million, and net income of -$1.1 million. Despite the record-high revenues, Metalla was unable to record positive operating cash flow, let alone net income. The company ended Q4 with cash of $9.7 million, and net debt of $3 million. For 2025, Metalla projects attributable production of 3,500-4,500 toz of gold equivalent, which means a notable improvement to the 2024 volume of 2,481 toz.

EMX Royalty reported Q4 revenues of $8.2 million, operating cash flow of $6.5 million, and net income of $1.8 million. All three numbers mean new record highs, if Q3 2023, when EMX received the delayed Timok royalty payments from Zijin Mining (OTCPK:ZIJMF), is not taken into account. EMX ended Q4 with cash of 32.8 million, and net debt of $1.7 million. According to the 2025 guidance, EMX expects sales of 10,00-12,000 toz of gold equivalent. This amount is lower than the 2014 sales of 13,897 toz, as EMX's notable exposure to copper reduces the volume of gold equivalent ounces at the current gold prices.

On March 26, EMX announced the commencement of a Normal Course Issuer Bid, under which EMX can repurchase for cancellation up to 5,440,027 shares (approximately 5% of issued and outstanding shares).

On March 27, EMX reported that at Zijin Mining's Timok mine, the copper and gold reserves and resources increased significantly. Moreover, Zijin discovered a new deposit called Malka Golaja, located 7 kilometers to the southeast of the Cukaru Peki mine. According to EMX, Malka Golaja is covered by its Brestovac royalty (0.3625% NSR royalty).

On March 31, EMX announced the granting of 840,500 incentive stock options exercisable for C$2.92 over a 5-year period, 841,000 restricted share units, and 116,000 deferred share units.

Vox Royalty provided some portfolio updates. The most important news is that Vox believes that several projects, upon which it holds royalties, will be fast-tracked to production over the coming 2–3 years. These include Orla Mining's (ORLA) South Railroad project or Black Cat Syndicate's (OTCPK:BLCAF) Kal East Gold project. Vox also announced the renewal of its share repurchase program. The company will be able to repurchase shares worth $1.5 million.

On March 20, Vox announced the commencement of mining at Catalyst Metals' (OTCPK:CTYMF) Plutonic East mine (Vox holds a grade-linked tonnage royalty) and Black Cat Syndicate's Boundary deposit (Vox holds a 1% NSR royalty).

Orogen Royalties announced preliminary Q4 revenues of C$4.3 million and preliminary net income of C$2.6 million. Both numbers mean a significant improvement compared to Q4 2023, by 79% and 63%, respectively.

Elemental Altus Royalties announced preliminary Q4 revenues of $6.8 million, which means a new record-high, by a wide margin. The preliminary 2024 sales volume is estimated at 8,990 toz of gold equivalent, which should be beaten in 2025, as the guidance promises 11,600-13,200 toz of gold equivalent. The adjusted revenues should increase by 50% in 2025.

On March 18, Elemental announced a normal course issuer bid, under which it will be able to purchase and cancel up to 12,288,129 shares. It means up to 5% of the issued and outstanding shares.

Empress Royalty announced that Luca Mining (OTCQX:LUCMF) reached commercial production at its Tahuehueto mine. Empress holds a 100% silver stream on Tahuehueto.

Star Royalties (OTCQX:STRFF) announced that Minera Alamos reissued a PEA for its Copperstone gold mine. Star Royalties expects that its 4% gold stream should generate revenues of over $20 million over the currently projected mine life. Moreover, Star owns 9.6 million shares of Minera Alamos.

Silver Crown Royalties reported that it received royalty payments from PPX Mining (OTCPK:SNNGF) and Gold Mountain Mining (OTCQB:GMTNF). The company also announced Q4 revenues of C$234,000. Although Q4 represented the fourth consecutive quarter of growing revenues, the numbers remain low. Also, the operating cash flow was only -$200,000, and the net income was -$300,000. The company ended Q4 with cash of $1.5 million, and debt-free.

On March 31, Silver Crown announced the closing of the second tranche of non-brokered private placement, by issuing 75,310 units for total proceeds of around C$489,515 ($352,893).

The April Outlook

The start of April has been pretty turbulent, due to the ongoing tariff wars that initiated a strong market-wide sell-off, followed by a steep recovery. For now, it seems that following the deferral of tariffs, the markets returned to the growth trajectory. Moreover, the gold price stormed to new all-time highs just below $3,250/toz. Should the political situation calm down, April could be another positive month for precious metals R&S companies. Although the huge March returns will be hard to replicate.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
